questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The equivalent weight of a certain trivalent element is $20$. Molecular weight of its oxide is
questions.solution:
Equivalent weight $=\frac{\text { molecular weight }}{\text { valence factor }}$
$\therefore$ Molecular weight $=20 \times 3=60$
It's oxide is $\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ and molecular weight
$$
=2 \times 60+16 \times 3=120+48=168
$$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.q: In the following reaction, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>→</mo><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math>
options: P is both oxidized as well as reduced
questions.solution:
4P0+3KOH+3H2O →3KH2P+1O2+P-3H3

It is a disproportionation reaction. Here P atom changes oxidation number zero to +1 and zero to -3. Hence, P is both oxidized as well as reduced

questions.q:
The reaction is balanced if,

       5 H 2 O 2 + XClO 2 + 2 OH - → XCl - + Y O 2 + 6 H 2 O
questions.solution: <math> <mrow> <mn>5</mn> <msub> <mtext>H</mtext> <mn>2</mn> </msub> <msub> <mtext>O</mtext> <mn>2</mn> </msub> <mo>+</mo> <mn>2</mn> <msub> <mtext>ClO</mtext> <mn>2</mn> </msub> <mo>+</mo> <mn>2</mn> <msup> <mtext>OH</mtext> <mo>-</mo> </msup> <mo>→</mo> <mn>2</mn> <msup> <mtext>Cl</mtext> <mo>-</mo> </msup> <mo>+</mo> <mn>5</mn> <msub> <mtext>O</mtext> <mn>2</mn> </msub> <mo>+</mo> <mn>6</mn> <msub> <mtext>H</mtext> <mn>2</mn> </msub> <mtext>O</mtext> </mrow> </math>

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: $0.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of an acid is neutralised by $40 \mathrm{cc}$ of $0.125 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{NaOH}$. The equivalent mass of the acid is
questions.solution:
Equivalent moles of $\mathrm{NaOH}=$ Equivalent moles of acid
$N_{1} V_{1}=\frac{\text { Weight }}{\text { Equivalent mass }}$ $40 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.125=\frac{0.5}{\text { Equivalent mass }}$ $\therefore$ Equivalent mass of acid $=100$

questions.q: Which one of the following is an example of disproportionation reaction?
questions.solution: A reaction in which the same species is simultaneously oxidised as well as reduced is called a disproportionation reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: While titration dilute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCI</mi></math> solution with aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math>, which of the following will not be required ?
questions.solution:
In this acid base Titrating there is no use of Bunsen burner and measuring cylinder other laboratory equipments will be required for getting the end point of titration.

These are the equipment required for titration.

  • Burette
  • Conical flask
  • Beaker
  • Pipette
  • Stirring rod
  • Small (filter) funnel
  • Burette stand and clamp (note 2)
  • White tile 
  • Indicator

questions.q:
If the molecular wt. of \(\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3\) and \(\mathrm{I}_2\) are \(\mathrm{M}_1\) and \(\mathrm{M}_2\) respectively, then what will be the equivalent wt. of \(\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3\) and \(\mathrm{I}_2\) in the following reaction?
\(2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3^{2-}+\mathrm{I}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{S}_4 \mathrm{O}_6^{2-}+2 \mathrm{I}^{-}\)
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: 4 g of copper was dissolved in concentrated nitric aci(d) The copper nitrate solution on strong heating gave $5 g$ of its oxide. The equivalent weight of copper is
questions.solution:
Copper on reacting with conc. HNO ${ }_{3}$ gives copper nitrate.

On strongly heating with copper nitrate, copper oxide is obtained.

$\therefore$ Equivalent weight of $\mathrm{Cu}$ $=$ Weight of copper $\times$ Equivalent weight of oxygen $=4 \times 8=32$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q:
For the reactions,
$\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2 ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-393 \mathrm{~J}$
$2 \mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{ZnO} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-412 \mathrm{~J}$
options: oxidation of carbon is not feasible
oxidation of Zn is not feasible
questions.solution: $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ negative shows that the reaction is spontaneous. Higher value for $\mathrm{Zn}$ shows that the reaction is more feasible.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q:
Balance the following equations in basic medium by ion - electron method and oxidation number methods

Cl 2 O 7 g + H 2 O 2 aq ⟶ ClO 2 - aq + O 2 g   stoichiometric coefficients for Cl2O7(g), H2O2(aq), ClO 2 - and O2(g) may be
questions.solution:
Cl + 7 2 O 7 + 8 e ⟶ 2 Cl + 3 O 2 - 1 RHR H 2 O 2 + 2 OH aq - 1 ⟶ O + 2 H 2 O + 2 e - OHR } × 4
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Cl 2 O 7 + 4 H 2 O 2 + 8 OH aq - ⟶ 2 ClO 2 aq - 1 + 4 O 2 + 8 H 2 O

To balance unbalanced oxygen atoms, add 3H2O molecules on LHS and 6 OH- ions on RHS

Cl 2 O 7 + 4 H 2 O 2 + 8 OH - + 3 H 2 O ⟶ 2 ClO 2 - 1 + 4 O 2 + 8 H 2 O + 6 OH aq - 1

Cl 2 O 7 + 4 H 2 O 2 + 2 OH aq - 1 ⟶ 2 ClO 2 - 1 + 4 O 2 + 5 H 2 O
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ reacts with $\mathrm{KI}$, in basic medium to form $\mathrm{I}_2$ and $\mathrm{MnO}_2$. When $250 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KI}$ solution is mixed with $250 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.02 \mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ in basic medium, what is the number of moles of $\mathrm{I}_2$ formed?
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{r}\lceil\text {-factor }=1 \longrightarrow \\ \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+\mathrm{I}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{MnO}_2+\mathrm{I}_2 \\ \bigsqcup_{n \text {-factor }=3}^{\rightleftharpoons}\end{array}$
Number of milli equivalents of
$\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}=0.02 \times 3 \times 250=15$
Number of milli equivalents of
$\mathrm{I}_2=0.1 \times 1 \times 250=25$
Thus, here $\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}$is limiting reagent.
$\therefore$ Number of milli equivalents of $\mathrm{I}_2$ formed $=$
Number of milli equivalent of $\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}=15$
or number of equivalent of $\mathrm{I}_2$ formed
$=\frac{15}{1000}=0.015$
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore \text { Number of moles of } \mathrm{I}_2 \text { formed } & =\frac{0.015}{2} \\
& =0.0075
\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

<math><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></math>

<math><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">w</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></math>

<math><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">w</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></math>
questions.solution:
The pH range between 3.1 (red) and 4.4 (yellow) is the colour-change interval of methyl orange.

Weak base has pH > 7. So, when methyl orange indicator is added the solution turns yellow & if the solution is titrated against strong acid it turns pinkish red at the end point i.e., pH <3.1.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A compound contains three elements X, Y and Z. The oxidation number of X, Y and Z are <math><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>, </mo><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and <math><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></math> respectively. The possible formula of the compound is
questions.solution:
The sum of all oxidation values must be equal to zero. Let, number of atoms of X, Y and Z are respectively a, b and c.

Then, (+3)a+(+5)b+(-2)c=0

Or

3a+5b-2c=0

Now, putting values of a, b and c for all the given options, which will give sum equal to zero, will be our formula.

(a) For, X2YZ

a=2

b=1

c=1

Thus, 3a+5b-2c=0

3×2+5-2≠0

(b) For, XYZ3

a=1,b=1,c=3

3a+5b-2c=0

3+5-6≠0

(c) X3(YZ4)3

a=3,b=3,c=12

3a+5b-2c=0

(3×3+5×3)-(2×12)=0

9+15-24

24-24=0

(d) X2Y2Z

a=2,b=2,c=1

3a+5b-2c=0

(3×2)+(5×2)-(2×1)=0

6+10-2≠0

Hence, option (c) is the correct answer.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Which of the following is disproportionation reaction?
questions.solution:
Reactions in which the same substance is oxidized as well as reduced are called disproportionation reaction. Writing the oxidation number of each element above its symbol in the given reactions.

C+4H4+1+2O20→C+4O2-2+2H2+1O-2

C-4H4+1+4Cl20→C+4Cl4+ 4 H +1 C −1 l

2F20+2O-2H-+1→2F-1+O+2F2-1+H2-1O-2

2N+4O2-2+2O-2H-+1→N+3O2--2+N+5O3--2+H2+1O-2

Thus, in the reaction of NO2 and OH-, N is both oxidized as well as reduced. Hence, the oxidation number increases from +4 in NO2 to +5 in NO3- and decreases from +4 in NO2 to +3 in NO2-.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an impure sample of magnesium carbonate (containing no thermally decomposable impurities) on complete thermal decomposition gave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>44</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of carbon dioxide gas. The percentage of impurity in the sample is
questions.solution:
Consider,

MgCO3 →MgO + CO2↑

MgCO3: 1×24+1×12+3×16=84 g

CO2: 

1×12+2×16=44 g

100% pure 84 g MgCO3 on heating gives 44 g CO2

Given that 1 g  of MgCO3 on heating gives  0.44 g CO2

Therefore, 84 g MgCO3 sample on heating gives 36.96 g CO2

Percentage of purity of the sample = 36.96 g of CO244 g of CO2× 100 %=84 %

Percentage of impurity = 16%
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Assuming complete ionization, same moles of which of the following compounds will require the least amount of acidified <math><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></math> for complete oxidation?
questions.solution:
MnO4-⟶Mn2+ ; Change in oxidation no. = 5

In option,

i. Fe2+ ⟶Fe3+

Change in oxidation no. = 1

ii. Fe2+ ⟶Fe3+

Change in oxidation no. = 1

SO32-⟶SO42-

Change in oxidation no. = 2

=1+2=3

iii. Fe2+ ⟶Fe3+

Change in oxidation no. = 1

C2O42-⟶2CO2

Change in oxidation no. = 2

=1+2=3

iv. Fe2+ ⟶Fe3+

Change in oxidation no. = 1

2NO2-⟶2NO3-

Change in oxidation no. = 4

=1+4=5
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
